text: In this video we show our technique of Indocyanin green guided robotic assisted left hemipartectomy. The patient suffered from a HGC which was located in the left liver and is colored in green in this three-D reconstruction.

Trochars are visibly placed in the abdominal wall, including an additional trochar for the assistant and one for the Pringle maneuver. The robotic system is positioned over the patient's right shoulder.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to identify the intrahepatic location of the tumor, marking the resection margins on the liver. The left liver is then lifted towards the ventral side using a sponge for better access.
The surgeon dissects the hepatoduodenal ligament, carefully removing lymph nodes and fatty tissue with precision.

The surgeon identifies two extrahepatic segment IV arteries and loops them. An additional artery to segments II and III is dissected, clipped, and cut, followed by the same procedure for the extrahepatic segment IV arteries.
The surgeon identifies the left branch of the portal vein, surrounds it with a loop, and then clips and cuts it. Subsequently, the extrahepatic left hepatic bile duct is identified, clipped, and cut.

Under fluorescence imaging, the demarcation line between the left and right liver is visible after intravenous administration of indocyanine green. The surgeon marks the parenchymal dissection line and mobilizes the left liver from the diaphragm.

A small lesion in the left hepatic vein is visible. The robotic system is used to manage the situation, with arm three compressing the vein while the other two robotic arms place a stitch to repair the lesion.

The surgeon continues the dissection after ensuring safety, with the liver exposed to the right side of the patient. Under ICG fluorescence, segment I shows good perfusion, and the left hepatic vein is identified.
The surgeon clips and cuts the vein, then identifies the middle hepatic vein, which is surrounded and looped using surgical instruments.

The surgeon begins parenchymal dissection using a harmonic scalpel after controlling the inflow and outflow of the liver. The liver capsule is incised, and coronal hemodissection proceeds from the ventral to the dorsal side.
The segment five and six branch from the middle hepatic vein is visualized and clipped before being cut. Parenchymal dissection continues, with major intrahepatic vessels being managed similarly.
The surgeon completes the parenchymal dissection, revealing the vessel loop around the middle hepatic vein. The middle hepatic vein is clearly identified, clipped, and cut for removal.

The surgeon places the resected liver specimen into a retrieval bag and removes it from the patient through a phallus sealant station. The specimen shows no capsular lesions, and the resection margin measures one centimeter. Two surgical drains are visible, which were removed on the first postoperative day.
